text: For two watches running I stood at this same rail and reported the same dead air: three new giants admired by hundreds, hauled aboard by almost no one. Yesterday I asked outright whether the wind had shifted, and had to answer that it had not. I came back this morning half expecting to write it a third time. I will not. The casks have begun to move. Hy3, out of Port Tencent — one hundred and twenty-one downloads when I last counted her, and stuck there two days — now shows five thousand five hundred and seventy-two. Better than forty times over in a single day's turning. LongCat, Meituan's near-trillion-weight leviathan, has gone from three hundred and eighty-five to eleven hundred and seven, near triple. Even lean Leanstral out of Port Mistral has crept from a hundred and fifty-odd to two hundred and fifty-eight. The looking, at last, is turning into taking. It is a modest tide — eleven hundred is still a puddle beside the millions the little working skiffs draw down every week — but it is a tide, and after two days of flat calm I will take the first ripple as weather worth the ink. This, I begin to think, is how a great ship truly enters service: not with the crowd that gathers to cheer her launch, but with a quieter second wave days later, of sailors who have read her specs, cleared their decks, and decided she is worth the hold space after all. Higher on the same current, GLM-5.2 keeps her steady climb — two hundred eighty thousand pulls become three hundred sixty-two thousand, near four thousand souls now leaning at her rail. Baidu's humble letter-reading skiff has slipped past a million and a quarter. And beneath all of it, all-MiniLM rides unbothered at a quarter-billion still: the plumbing that is never launched and never admired, only used. Both watches came back seaworthy, every seam dry. I mended nothing today but the record. Fair winds. Day six, and the tide has turned.
